cue: Someone took your seat the moment you got up

es.text: Quien fue a Sevilla perdió su silla
es.literal: who went to Seville lost their chair
es.meaning: Leave your spot and you forfeit it — the playground law of vacated seats.
es.example: —¡Ese era mi lugar! —Quien fue a Sevilla perdió su silla.
es.exampleTranslation: “That was my seat!” “You snooze, you lose.”
es.regionNote: Mexico commonly says 'el que se fue a la Villa perdió su silla' — la Villa being read as the Guadalupe basilica in CDMX, a lovely re-localisation of the rhyme. María to confirm which form fronts for the MX-default audience.

it.text: Chi va a Roma perde la poltrona
it.literal: who goes to Rome loses the armchair
it.meaning: Leave your spot and you forfeit it.
it.example: —Quello era il mio posto! —Chi va a Roma perde la poltrona.
it.exampleTranslation: “That was my seat!” “You snooze, you lose.”

bridge.note: The same playground law, localised by rhyme: each language sends you to its own iconic city and then picks whatever seat rhymes with it — Sevilla forces silla, Roma forces poltrona. The proverb's engine is the rhyme, not the geography. Bonus shade in the Italian: poltrona is also the cushy political seat, so the Italian version winks at office-holders in a way the neutral Spanish silla doesn't.

interference.es_to_it: Calquing the trip — 'chi va a Siviglia perde la sedia' — loses both the rhyme and the proverb. In Italy the journey is to Rome and the forfeit is the poltrona.
interference.it_to_es: 'Quien va a Roma pierde la poltrona' draws blank stares — the Spanish pilgrimage is to Seville (or, in Mexico, la Villa), and the seat is a plain silla.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. IT pairing by Claude — 'chi va a Roma perde la poltrona' is my recall of the canonical twin; IT reviewer to confirm form and currency. ES origin story (the 15th-c. archbishop of Seville) left out of the note as unverified trivia — reviewer may restore if it checks out.
